drills.scenario: FitForge, a local gym, runs a lead-gen campaign for free trial passes. Target CPL is $8. Sara opens Ads Manager and one ad set shows the HIGHEST cost per lead ($14), the LOWEST CTR, and the HIGHEST CPC. She's about to pause it on the spot.
drills.question: Before pausing, what should Sara check?
options: Nothing — high CPL, low CTR, and high CPC together clearly mean it's a loser, pause immediately
The downstream sales and revenue that ad set's leads actually generated
Whether the ad's image is black-and-white or color
The campaign's lifetime CPM trend
drills.explanation: Don't kill expensive leads without checking lead quality (Ch. 29). In the book's own example the ad set with the highest lead cost, lowest CTR, and highest CPC drove the MOST sales and revenue. Always cross-reference downstream sales/revenue before pausing — the most expensive leads can be the highest quality.

drills.scenario: Pawsome, a Shopify pet-treats brand, has a website conversion campaign with target CPA (cost per sale) of $20. After 7 days, Liam sees CTR is down and CPM is up week-over-week, so he panics that the account is collapsing — even though sales are still coming in at $18 per sale.
drills.question: What's the core mistake Liam is making?
options: He should optimize for impressions instead of conversions
He's reading the wrong metric — CTR/CPM are secondary diagnostics, CPA is the primary success metric
He needs to switch to a 1-day conversion window
He should pause the whole campaign and rebuild it
drills.explanation: Make CPA your primary success metric (Ch. 29). CTR/CPC/CPM are secondary, diagnostic metrics — useful to troubleshoot WHERE a funnel breaks, but reading them instead of CPA makes you think ads are tanking when CPA ($18 vs $20 target) says they're winning. Look at the wrong metric and you read the wrong story.

drills.scenario: Coach Mara sells a webinar and her registration campaign's cost per registration has crept far above her $12 target. She wants to systematically cut the losers using the book's pausing workflow.
drills.question: Put the steps to fix high CPAs by pausing ad sets in the correct order.
options: Know your target CPA
Sort your reporting view by the last seven days
Sort ad sets by cost of conversion, greatest to least
Pause every ad set converting at 2-3x your target CPA
Sort ad sets by Amount Spent (greatest to least) and turn off any that spent 2x your CPA with zero conversions
drills.explanation: Fixing High CPAs by Pausing Ad Sets: first know your target CPA, narrow the view to the last 7 days, sort by cost of conversion to find the worst converters and pause those at 2-3x target, then catch the zero-converters by sorting on Amount Spent and killing any ad set that spent 2x CPA with zero conversions.

drills.scenario: A new media buyer at Pawsome wants to read and diagnose results in Ads Manager the right way, working from the big picture down to the root cause rather than jumping around randomly.
drills.question: Order the top-down 'Read & Diagnose Results in Ads Manager' workflow (Ch. 29).
options: Review the default Performance view at the CAMPAIGN level (Results + Cost per Result)
Drill into the Ad Set level to judge which ad sets perform well vs. poorly
Customize columns (e.g. 'Performance and clicks' preset) to understand WHY
Cross-reference several metrics together rather than in isolation
Use the Breakdown menu to segment by age, gender, country
Export to Excel for deeper sorting and additional calculations
drills.explanation: Ch. 29 workflow is top-down: start at the campaign-level Performance view, drill into ad sets, customize columns to learn WHY, cross-reference metrics together (never in isolation), then use Breakdown to find sub-audiences, and finally export to Excel when data volume is high.

drills.scenario: Coach Mara reports her webinar ad got 'reach 200,000' but '1,400,000 impressions.' Her business partner says 'Wow, 1.4 million people saw it!' and wants to scale immediately.
drills.question: How should Mara correct her partner?
options: He's right — impressions and reach both count unique people
Impressions are opportunities to see and can repeat; reach is unique people — so ~200K people saw it an average of 7 times each
Reach counts every view including repeats, so only impressions matter
The numbers must be wrong because impressions can't exceed reach
drills.explanation: Confusing impressions with people is a classic pitfall. Impressions = opportunities to see (can repeat); reach = unique people. 1.4M impressions / 200,000 reach = an average of 7 exposures per person, not 1.4M people. Note frequency ~7 is also creeping toward the fatigue zone (~10+).

drills.scenario: FitForge's gym campaign drives clicks OFF Facebook to a landing page. The new intern brags that 'CTR is 4.2%!' but the link clicks and landing page traffic look surprisingly low for that CTR.
drills.question: What's the most likely explanation?
options: The intern is reading CTR (All), which counts reactions, comments, shares, and profile clicks — not just link clicks
The pixel is broken so link clicks aren't recorded
CTR can't be measured for off-Facebook traffic
The landing page is loading too slowly
drills.explanation: Using CTR (All) for off-Facebook traffic is a known pitfall. CTR (All) counts reactions, comments, shares, and profile clicks, which inflates the number. For ads driving traffic off Facebook, use CTR (Link Click-Through Rate). A high link CTR rule of thumb is >1%.

drills.scenario: Before testing audiences for the gym, the FitForge buyer wants to lock in the strongest creative first so audience results aren't muddied by weak creative (Sequenced Optimization, Ch. 11).
drills.question: Order the Sequenced Optimization steps: win the image, then the copy, then the audience.
options: Create ONE ad set with 2-3 ads that differ ONLY by image
Let them run a few days; identify the best-performing image
Duplicate the winner and split-test two ad COPY versions using that same winning image
With optimal image + optimal copy locked, begin testing your audience
Wait to see how the first ad set performs before building additional ad sets to save money
drills.explanation: Sequenced Optimization (Ch. 11): win the image first (one ad set, ads differing only by image), then win the copy (duplicate winner, split-test copy on the winning image), and only THEN test audiences with creative already locked. Build additional ad sets only after seeing how the first performs, to save money.

drills.scenario: Pawsome's pixel reports that Campaign #1 (a top-of-funnel video) got ZERO attributed conversions, while six other campaigns got sales. A junior analyst recommends killing Campaign #1 entirely as 'a complete waste.'
drills.question: What's the risk in that recommendation?
options: No risk — zero attributed conversions always means zero effect
Campaign #1 may have influenced/assisted the other six campaigns' conversions; assist effects are real
The pixel must be misconfigured if any campaign shows zero
Video campaigns should always be judged on view count, never conversions
drills.explanation: Assuming a zero-conversion campaign had no effect is one of the costliest analysis mistakes. A campaign with zero attributed conversions may still have influenced the others — cross-channel/assist effects are real. Don't kill it purely on last-touch attribution without considering its upstream contribution.

drills.scenario: Coach Mara is setting up a website conversion campaign that needs to track BOTH webinar registrations (leads) and the downstream paid course sales. She wants the custom column view the book recommends (Ch. 29) so she can diagnose the full funnel.
drills.question: Which columns should be in her custom view? (Select all that apply.)
options: No. of Leads (Results) and Cost Per Lead
CTR (Link Clicks) and Link Clicks
CPC (Cost per Link Click)
Number of Sales and Sales Conversion Value
Relevance Score history for every competitor's ad
drills.explanation: Ch. 29 custom column view for a lead-gen conversion campaign measuring both leads and sales: No. of Leads, Cost Per Lead, CTR (Link Clicks), Link Clicks, CPC, Number of Sales, and Sales Conversion Value. Competitor Relevance Score history isn't available or part of the view.

drills.scenario: FitForge wants to know whether a new lookalike audience converts QUICKLY for a flash 3-day trial promo, so they can decide fast. The default 7-day click window will pull more data over time, but Sara needs an answer within the promo's short life.
drills.question: Which conversion window choice fits this situation best?
options: Use a 7-day click window because the authors always prefer it with no exceptions
Use a 1-day window to test whether the audience converts quickly
Turn off attribution entirely for the test
Use 28-day click to maximize attributed conversions
drills.explanation: Choose the conversion window deliberately (Ch. 26). Use a 1-day window to test whether an audience/creative converts quickly; use 7-day click when you have time (algorithm pulls more data, often better over time). The authors prefer 7-day but always TEST 1-day vs 7-day — and a fast 3-day promo is exactly the 1-day case.

drills.scenario: A Pawsome boosted blog post from last year quietly drove $6,730 in revenue on $347 spend. The buyer wants to mine existing account data for a proven hook to build a new cold campaign around (Ch. 16).
drills.question: Which metric should be the PRIMARY driving signal when mining for a hidden hook?
options: CPM, because it shows cheap reach
Link clicks — people click the link when the subject resonates
Relevance Score alone
Total amount spent, highest to lowest
drills.explanation: Facebook the Hook (Ch. 16): use link clicks as the driving metric because people click the link when the subject resonates. CTR, CPC, and Relevance Score are secondary. Sorting link clicks highest-to-lowest surfaces hooks the customer has already proven in their own data.
